title: Approach to investigation and management of proteinuria in pregnancy
one_line_summary: This review outlines renal adaptations in pregnancy, diagnostic criteria for proteinuria, and systematic management of proteinuric disorders, including new-onset proteinuria, angiogenic biomarkers, and renal biopsy indications.
key_pearls: Never use eGFR to assess renal function in a pregnant patient; monitor serial serum creatinine, as a level > 90 micromol/L represents significant renal impairment in pregnancy.
Initiate aspirin 75-150 mg daily at 12 weeks' gestation for pregnant patients with CKD, pre-existing hypertension, autoimmune disease, or a history of hypertensive disorders of pregnancy.

For any pregnant patient with nephrotic-range proteinuria (uPCR > 300 mg/mmol or uACR > 250 mg/mmol), immediately initiate prophylactic-dose LMWH throughout gestation and for 6 weeks postpartum to mitigate thromboembolism risk; avoid salt restriction and routine diuretics.

Limit renal biopsy to the first or early second trimester, reserving it for cases of unexplained rapidly progressive glomerulonephritis, unexplained nephrotic syndrome, or suspected transplant rejection where management would change.

sections.heading: Renal Physiology and Normal Protein Excretion in Pregnancy
sections.content: Pregnancy induces systemic vasodilation and plasma volume expansion, leading to a 50% increase in Glomerular Filtration Rate (GFR). Tubular function and protein handling are altered, resulting in enhanced protein excretion. These physiological changes can persist postpartum, taking 5 to 6 months to return to pre-pregnancy baselines. In healthy non-pregnant individuals, urinary protein excretion is less than 150 mg/24 h. In healthy pregnancies, excretion can rise but should not exceed 300 mg/24 h. A prospective study of 270 healthy pregnant women established the 95th percentile (upper limit of normal) of protein excretion as 260 mg/day, corresponding to a spot uPCR of 26 mg/mmol.

sections.heading: Definition, Detection, and Kidney Function Assessment
sections.content: Proteinuria is defined as more than 300 mg of protein in a 24-hour urine collection, or a spot urine protein:creatinine ratio (uPCR) of more than 30 mg/mmol. 24-hour urine collections are no longer routinely required or recommended for standard quantification. Estimated GFR (eGFR) equations (e.g., CKD-EPI, MDRD) are invalid and must not be used during pregnancy. Instead, serial measurements of raw serum creatinine must be used. In a healthy pregnancy, serum creatinine falls during the first trimester, reaches a nadir and plateaus in the second trimester, gradually rises during the third trimester, and returns to the pre-pregnancy baseline by approximately 18 weeks postpartum.

sections.heading: Nephrotic Range Proteinuria in Pregnancy
sections.content: Nephrotic range proteinuria is defined as a urine albumin:creatinine ratio (uACR) of more than 2.5 g/24 h (more than 250 mg/mmol) or a uPCR of more than 3 g/24 h (more than 300 mg/mmol). Key management principles include: - Salt restriction must be avoided in pregnancy to prevent intravascular volume depletion. - Serum albumin is unreliable as an isolated marker of nephrosis severity due to physiological hemodilution. - Thromboprophylaxis is mandatory; offer prophylactic-dose low-molecular-weight heparin (LMWH) antenatally, continuing for at least 6 weeks postnatally. A formal birth plan is required. - Diuretics are not routinely recommended due to the risk of placental hypoperfusion and oligohydramnios; use only on a case-by-case basis under joint obstetric medicine/nephrology guidance. - Immunosuppression: Calcineurin inhibitors (tacrolimus, cyclosporin), azathioprine, and prednisolone are generally safe. Rituximab can be used in early pregnancy but must be avoided in the second and third trimesters to prevent neonatal B-cell depletion. Patients on steroids or calcineurin inhibitors have an elevated risk of gestational diabetes and must undergo an oral glucose tolerance test (OGTT). Prognostic Impact: In pre-eclamptic singleton pregnancies, new-onset proteinuria of 300 to 499 mg/24 h is associated with severe hypertension, earlier delivery, and fetal growth restriction (FGR). Proteinuria exceeding 500 mg/24 h carries a significantly higher risk of preterm delivery, cesarean section, and the need for magnesium sulfate therapy.

sections.heading: Pre-eclampsia (PET)
sections.content: Pre-eclampsia (PET) is diagnosed by new-onset hypertension (systolic BP >= 140 mmHg or diastolic BP >= 90 mmHg on at least two occasions) arising after 20 weeks' gestation, accompanied by one or more of the following: - New-onset proteinuria (Note: 10% of PET cases do not present with proteinuria; its presence is not mandatory for diagnosis). - Maternal organ dysfunction: Kidney impairment (serum creatinine > 90 micromol/L); liver involvement (ALT or AST > 2 times the upper limit of normal, with or without right upper quadrant/epigastric pain); neurological complications (eclampsia, altered mental status, visual disturbances, stroke, myoclonus, papilledema, severe headache); hematological complications (platelets < 150,000/microliter, disseminated intravascular coagulation [DIC], or hemolysis). - Uteroplacental dysfunction: Fetal growth restriction (FGR < 10th centile), abnormal umbilical artery Dopplers, placental abruption, or stillbirth. Risk Factors & Prophylaxis (NICE Guidelines): - High-Risk Factors: Pre-existing hypertension, chronic kidney disease (CKD), diabetes mellitus, connective tissue disorders (e.g., SLE), anti-phospholipid syndrome, or hypertensive disease in a previous pregnancy. - Moderate-Risk Factors: Maternal age > 40 years, nulliparity, multiple pregnancy, pregnancy interval > 10 years, family history of PET (first-degree relative), BMI > 35 kg/m2 at booking, or IVF (especially with donor eggs). - Aspirin Prophylaxis: Patients with 1 or more high-risk factors or more than 1 moderate-risk factor should take aspirin 75 to 150 mg once daily from 12 weeks until 36 weeks' gestation. Serial fetal growth scans are indicated at 28, 32, and 36 weeks. Angiogenic Biomarkers (sFlt-1/PlGF): Used between 24 and 36 weeks plus 6 days of gestation to rule out or risk-stratify suspected PET. - PlGF Levels: < 12 pg/mL is highly abnormal (severe placental dysfunction, high risk of preterm delivery); 12 to 100 pg/mL is abnormal; >= 100 pg/mL is normal (delivery within 14 days is highly unlikely). - sFlt-1/PlGF Ratio Risk Stratification: Ratio <= 38: Low risk (0.4% risk of PET within 7 days; 1.2% within 14 days). Ratio > 38 and <= 85: Moderate risk (20% risk of PET within 7 days; 26.7% within 14 days). Ratio > 85: High risk (54.7% risk of PET within 7 days; 67.9% within 14 days).

sections.heading: Pre-existing Hypertension and Gestational Proteinuria
sections.content: Pre-existing hypertension is defined as BP >= 140/90 mmHg diagnosed before pregnancy or before 20 weeks' gestation. Target BP is <= 135/85 mmHg. ACE inhibitors and ARBs must be discontinued prior to conception or immediately upon a positive pregnancy test due to teratogenicity. Safe alternatives include labetalol, nifedipine, amlodipine, methyldopa, doxazosin, and hydralazine. Superimposed PET Detection: Suspect PET after 20 weeks if there is a doubling of antihypertensive doses, or a doubling of baseline uPCR/uACR. PlGF testing is useful but must be interpreted cautiously in patients with renal impairment, as PlGF is renally cleared and may be artificially low. Gestational Proteinuria: Isolated proteinuria arising at or after 20 weeks' gestation in the absence of hypertension or systemic features of PET or primary kidney disease. It is a retrospective diagnosis, typically resolving by 3 months postpartum. However, 13% to 25% of women who eventually develop PET initially present with isolated gestational proteinuria, necessitating close surveillance. Repeat uPCR and BP at 12 weeks postpartum; refer to nephrology if proteinuria persists.

sections.heading: Chronic Kidney Disease (CKD) in Pregnancy
sections.content: Chronic Kidney Disease (CKD) in pregnancy carries an increased risk of superimposed PET, FGR, preterm birth, neonatal ICU admission, and cesarean section. Pregnancy can permanently accelerate the decline of maternal kidney function, particularly in CKD stages 3a to 5. Patients with uncontrolled SLE and even mild baseline renal impairment are at risk of precipitous renal decline. Predictors of Renal Decline: Difficult-to-control hypertension, heavy baseline proteinuria, and a failure of serum creatinine to fall by at least 10% during the first trimester.

sections.heading: Diagnostic Work-up and Indications for Kidney Biopsy
sections.content: De Novo Proteinuria < 20 Weeks: Represents pre-existing, previously undiagnosed kidney disease. Work-up includes: - Serology/Immunology: PLA2R antibodies (if new nephrotic syndrome), viral screen (HIV, Hep B, Hep C), HbA1c (HbA1c > 48 mmol/mol in the first trimester indicates pre-existing diabetes), and a renal acute screen if persistent microscopic hematuria (>= 3+ blood on dipstick) is present (ANCA, ANA, double-stranded DNA, complement levels C3/C4, immunoglobulins, rheumatoid factor). - Imaging: Renal ultrasound to assess kidney size, morphology, and signs of chronicity. Lupus Flare vs. PET: A lupus nephritis flare is characterized by rising creatinine/proteinuria accompanied by pancytopenia, rising dsDNA, falling complement levels, and elevated CRP. Indications for Renal Biopsy: Biopsy is performed in the first or early second trimester *only* if the histological diagnosis will directly alter clinical management. Indications include: 1. New-onset nephrotic syndrome, rapidly deteriorating kidney function, or hematoproteinuric acute kidney injury (AKI) of unclear etiology. 2. To guide immunosuppressive therapy or plasma exchange to safely prolong the pregnancy to at least 32 weeks. 3. Suspected acute allograft rejection in a kidney transplant recipient.

strengths_limitations: The review provides highly practical, clear diagnostic thresholds and integrates obstetric guidelines (NICE) with nephrology guidelines (KDIGO, UKKA). A limitation is that PlGF-based testing, while highly useful, has reduced diagnostic specificity in patients with advanced CKD due to altered renal clearance of the biomarker.
